title: Chapter 8
text: Chapter Five L ara was pissed by the time they reached her home. She didn’t wait for the bot to stop before leaping out of its arms, splashing mud all over her legs when she landed. The toes of her bare foot sank in the muck, but she marched on, refusing to acknowledge her discomfort. Water poured over the edge of the roof to land on her already drenched head when she stopped at the door . Swearing under her breath, she shoved the door aside and stomped into the shack. She threw her broken boot into a corner, dropped her bag on the pallet, and spun on the bot, thrusting a finger at it before it entered . “Don’t you ever do that again, you son of a bitch .” “Not the son of anything .” “Don’t get smart with me. You had no right handling me that way .” “I have every right to ensure our agreement is fulfilled .” “We don’t have an ag—What are you doing?” She retreated to maintain the distance between them as it walked inside. “I didn’t invite you in !” “I’m not going to stand outside, in the rain, while I make my offer .” She glared at it, gaze moving from its face to trek over its body. The way it looked and moved, it was so damned human . She’d even felt its warmth through their clothing while it held her. If it weren’t for its metal hands, she would never have guessed its true nature . Lara reminded herself of the key difference. Most humans killed for good reason — survival. But these things killed because they were stronger; they killed because they could . Bots didn’t have a conscience. It didn’t matter if this one had voiced concern for her, had offered to bandage the scrape on her knee, had carried her home so she didn’t injure herself further … She leaned down, pulled off her intact boot, and tossed it near its mate. Not without hesitation, she turned her back on the bot and crossed the space to the lantern. It took several frustrating attempts to produce a flame with the lighter, but the soft, familiar glow was a relief. Inhaling deeply, she tucked the lighter away and returned her attention to the bot . “Shit!” She started, hand flying to her chest. The bot stood directly behind her. She hadn’t even heard it move . It was silent, eyes on her. The intensity of its unblinking stare reminded her again how different they were. How dangerous it was . Glancing down, she noticed it was holding out the bundle of food. Hunger decided to reintroduce itself in that moment, churning her stomach. She snatched the food from the bot’s hand . “I’m not going to say thank you,” she said around a mouthful of meat . The burst of flavor had her salivating. She took another bite before she finished the first . “You don’t have to. Just listen .” “Lihshing.” So. Damn . Good . When the bot said no more, Lara looked up at it. It still had its watchful stare fixed on her. The rain drummed on the roof, the sound competing with that of her chewing . “What?” she asked, wadding the food in one cheek . “When was the last time you ate ?” “ Why ?” “If it’s been a while, you’ll make yourself sick .” “Screw you. I don’t tell you where to shove your oil, or whatever. I’ll eat how I want.” She took another bite, chewing with her mouth open to display the food. If the bot said a single damned thing about her manners, she’d spit the meat right in its face. It’d be worth the waste of food . Almost . The bot stared for a little longer, and, finally, turned away. “I’ll wait until you’re done .” Lara scoffed and moved to her pallet. Just before her ass settled atop it, she remembered that she was soaked. Scowling, she straightened back up and continued to eat, savoring the flavor despite her ravenousness . She hadn’t eaten in two days . With nothing else to look at, her eyes wandered to the bot. Its attention was on her collection of treasures. Without its penetrating stare upon her, she was free to study it . It wore a faded olive coat, repaired in more places than she could count by patches and neat, tight stitches. The only unrepaired damage were the three small holes on its chest. Its pack was slung over one shoulder, and she assumed its rifle was over the other, bundled in a protective bag. Her bravado would have burned out a lot faster if she’d noticed that while she brandished her knife earlier . The bot’s boots were well-worn, but had been meticulously cared for — unlike her own. She couldn’t bear to look back at her footwear, knowing that one boot lay on its side, sole hanging open in something too close to a frown . “Why do you keep these things?” the bot asked . “What are you talking about ?” “They serve no purpose .” “I thought they were pretty,” she said defensively, looking over her collection . It reminded her of what she’d found earlier. Folding the remaining bit of meat into her mouth, she opened her bag and removed the frame. Fortunately, though the bag itself was damp, the photo hadn’t gotten wet. As she carried the frame toward the shelves, she noticed the bot holding the porcelain shard between its fingers, brow furrowed as it examined the item. The bot seemed… confused . Lara placed the photo on the top shelf, leaning the frame against the wall. Stepping back, she observed it as a piece of her larger collection, beautiful despite the damage the frame had taken . “That world is lost to all of us,” the bot said . “No shit,” Lara replied, clenching her jaw so she didn’t show how deeply his words affected her. Just another reminder of how desolate her life was. This here — this lonely shack — was as good as things would ever be. “Say what you wanted to say so you can get out of here .” The bot was quiet, staring at the photo for what felt like years. Its body was too close to hers, too unmoving; no rise and fall of its chest, no shifting of its feet. It was unnatural . “You need food. Clean water. Reliable shelter,” it finally said. She intended to reply with another no shit , but the bot’s next words stole her breath. “I can give you all that .” “Just give it?” she asked when she regained her composure. “Just like that? Nothing is free .” “You’re right. Forgive my choice of words. It would be an arrangement .” “Look, I don’t know how fried your processor got out in the Dust, but I’ve already told you, I don’t — ” The bot lifted its hand, holding up a single finger. Lara snapped her mouth shut, glaring at him . “Our current arrangement is that you will listen,” it said, meeting her gaze. Its eyes were so impossibly green. “I will provide food, water, shelter, clothing — any and all of the things your body requires to function. You will quarter at my residence—” it leveled its finger at her, silencing the protest she was about to make, “—for the duration of this agreement. The price is simple, and one you can easily pay .” “No.” She didn’t care about their arrangement, didn’t want an agreement . There was no way she’d become a bot’s plaything. The very thought sent a shiver along her spine, threatening to stir up memories of cruel, inhuman hands on her body . The bot was silent, staring at her with expressionless eyes as her heart thumped . “Dance for me,” it finally said. “That’s the only payment I ask .” “God dammit, I said I don’t do that anymore! Go to Kitty’s .” “I did. It was… unsatisfying .” Lara’s brows fell. Unsatisfying? There were bot and human dancers there, all beautiful. They were seductive, sensual, and — unlike Lara — offered private couplings for a price. How could a place like that not satisfy whatever cravings this thing had ? She paused, thinking over its words again. The more she thought about them, the more human they seemed . “What do you mean ?” “I went and watched. None of them danced like you .” “So, you just decided — after seeing me dance for a few seconds — that you want me as your own, personal little dancer in your house ?” “ Yes .” She waited for it to say more, to elaborate, but it seemed to think that one word was enough . Lara lifted a leg to scratch at the dried mud on her calf. Despite the meat she’d wolfed down, hunger gnawed at the pit of her stomach. This bot was offering everything she could ever need, the first comfort of her entire life, and she was tempted to refuse again . Tabitha had worked double shifts after Lara left Kitty’s, just to keep the two of them fed. Even knowing what had happened to Lara, Tabitha still succumbed to a similar offer from another bot. Warmth and a full belly beat having perverts pawing at your tits and ass every night . “What’s your name?” the bot asked . She pushed aside the guilt that always accompanied thoughts of Tabitha . “ Lara .” “Is that all ?” “What the hell do you mean, is that all ?” “Humans usually have two or three names. Is it just Lara ?” “Oh. Brooks. Lara Brooks.” She tugged at the wet fabric around her neck, loosening it. “What’s… yours ?” “I told you yesterday.” Was there a hint of offense in its tone? That wasn’t possible; bots didn’t work that way . “I was a little scared, okay? I wasn’t really paying attention to names and all .” “Do most humans threaten violence when they’re a little scared ?” “Yes, actually,” she replied, folding her arms across her chest. Especially the ones who’ve had bad experiences with bots. “So, you gonna tell me your name again or not ?” It tilted its head, never taking its eyes off her. Dread balled in her gut, a crushing weight; what was this thing going to do ? “Ronin,” it said . It sounded familiar, now that he mentioned it. “So now we’ve officially met, or whatever. The answer is still no. You can leave .” It blinked. “Look around you, Lara Brooks .” She pressed her lips together and looked around. Stupid , she scolded herself. The thing was just reminding her how shitty her life was . “I’m only asking you to dance for me. No more advances from other humans , no — ” “You saw that , huh ?” “You weren’t very aware of your surroundings this morning. Neither was he .” “ Obviously .” “You’ll be safe with me. All for doing something you enjoy .” Lara bit her tongue to keep from scoffing. Safe with a bot? They were the last things she’d ever feel safe with. Yet here she stood, in the home she and Tabitha had built together, having a conversation with one. Though Ronin had put his hands on her against her will, he hadn’t hurt her, hadn’t forced himself upon her. He wasn’t bullying her into an arrangement…he was attempting to be persuasive . When the hell did she start thinking of this bot as him ? “I will consider it with a couple conditions .” He nodded . “I will be dancing . Nothing more. You will not touch me .” “ What else ?” “I want your help finding my sister .” “You don’t know where she is ?” “Would I be asking for help if I did?” She took a steadying breath. This was a negotiation, now. Calm. “She was taken in by a bot, just like you’re trying to do with me. I haven’t seen her in weeks .” “Individuals who disappear in this world aren’t usually found .” “Don’t say that!” She stepped closer, tilting her head back to stare up at him . “It’s a fact .” Without thinking, she swung her hand at him. Before her palm made contact, one of his metal hands caught her wrist, holding it firm . “Do you have any other conditions?” Ronin’s expression was unchanged . Lara yanked on her arm. He didn’t squeeze, and his grip didn’t hurt, but she couldn’t break free of it . “If you’re not going to look for her, the answer is no .” “Never said I wouldn’t look. Are those all your terms ?” “You will not talk like that about her again,” she replied through clenched teeth . “I didn’t talk about her .” “You know what I mean !” “No, I don’t. I simply stated a fact .” “I don’t care about your facts. She’s not dead !” Ronin seemed to hesitate. “I will not touch you,” he said, releasing her wrist, “unless it’s necessary to your safety. I will help you look for her. And I will not talk like that about her again. You will dance for me, and I will provide all that is necessary for your survival. Are we agreed ?” Lara looked away from him, rubbing her wrist. Why hadn’t he struck her in retaliation ? “Yes.” She swallowed, mouth suddenly dry. Lara had sworn to herself she would never do anything like this. For the second time, she was trading herself to a bot . “Gather the items you wish to bring.” He glanced at the picture again, a shadow of movement flickering over his brow. “We’ll leave as soon as you’re ready .” That quickly? She picked up her bag and filled it with her treasures, setting them inside gently. She didn’t care if they were useless; they were hers. Once they were packed, she stood in front of the empty shelves, numb. Wasn’t there more? Wasn’t there anything else to her life here ? Rather than dwell on those thoughts, she stuffed her spare wraps into the bag and retrieved her boots from the corner. She frowned at the sagging sole . “We’ll repair them.” Ronin knelt in front of her and dropped his pack on the floor between his legs. Uncertain, Lara watched him rummage through it and pull out a long strip of cloth. There were singe marks on it, as though it had survived a fire . “Wrap that around your boot. Should hold it together for now, even if it won’t keep your foot dry .” “Um, thanks,” she said. Sitting on the edge of the pallet, she pulled her boots on. Her feet squished into them; they were as soaked as her clothes. She wrapped the cloth around her damaged boot, tying it tightly on top. When she stood, it fit strangely, but it was better than hurting her foot when she walked . Lastly, she crossed to the lantern. She was leaving the only home she’d known since she was a little girl. Where were the tears, the heartache? The answer came to her as she extinguished the wick . Without Tabitha, this place was nothing but another survival tool . “Done,” she whispered . Wordlessly, Ronin stepped outside. Lara followed, stopping just beyond the threshold. Standing on her toes, she removed the chimes from their hook and put them in her bag. The lines would undoubtedly get tangled, but she couldn’t leave it behind. This was a thing she’d made with her own hands. Besides, sorting out the mess of fishing lines would give her something to do . She felt Ronin’s eyes on her . As soon as she closed her pack, he walked. The rain continued, though it had eased significantly, raindrops making little ripples in the puddles. He seemed to lead her around the deepest puddles and the worst patches of mud, but her boots sloshed in the soft, hungry ground nonetheless . She couldn’t bring herself to look back at the shack . Would it still be there, waiting, when the bot was through with her ?
